skill.name: convex-setup-auth
skill.description: Sets up authentication for Convex applications with provider selection, identity mapping, protected backend functions, and access control guidance.
skill.summary: Configure Convex authentication with supported providers and secure backend authorization checks.
skill.author: get-convex

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many alerts, but review shows the content is primarily markdown guidance for Convex authentication setup. The command, network, and environment findings are mostly legitimate setup instructions, with medium residual risk because the skill may run CLIs, modify project files, and handle provider secrets. No prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, hidden code execution, or confirmed weak cryptography implementation was found.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate command execution guidance for auth setup
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run package installs and provider CLIs such as Convex Auth, Convex dev, Auth0 CLI, and Convex environment commands. This is expected for an auth setup skill, but it can modify a project and contact third-party services, so users should review commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced markdown explicitly names commands an agent may run. The context is legitimate setup workflow, not hidden execution or command injection.

medium_findings.title: Credential and environment configuration handling
medium_findings.description: The references list provider API keys, client IDs, secret keys, cookie passwords, and local environment files. This is necessary for authentication setup, but it increases risk if secrets are copied into chat, committed, or mixed between environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a direct list of secret-bearing configuration names and environment handling steps. I did not find evidence that the skill exfiltrates or exposes those secrets.

low_findings.title: Official documentation and dashboard URLs are benign network references
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Convex, Clerk, Auth0, and WorkOS documentation or dashboards. They support setup instructions and are not evidence of covert network access or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or provider setup pages. No suspicious destination or hidden payload was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography and reconnaissance scanner alerts appear to be false positives
low_findings.description: Static alerts on weak cryptography and reconnaissance were triggered by auth-related prose, YAML metadata, and ordinary setup wording. I found no hashing implementation, cryptographic primitive use, host enumeration, or system probing logic in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain descriptions, URLs, or setup language rather than executable cryptography or reconnaissance code. This strongly supports a false-positive classification.

low_findings.title: Browser storage mention is a documented Auth0 caveat
low_findings.description: The Auth0 reference discusses local storage in the context of a known refresh-token validation problem and tells the agent not to overstate success. This is a cautionary note, not an instruction to steal browser data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The nearby text frames local storage as an Auth0 configuration caveat and validation limitation. No code reads browser storage or transmits stored data.

content.value_statement: Adding authentication to Convex apps can require provider-specific wiring, secure identity checks, and environment configuration. This skill guides setup for Convex Auth, Clerk, WorkOS AuthKit, Auth0, and custom JWT providers.
actual_capabilities: Helps choose an auth provider before writing implementation code.
Guides setup for Convex Auth, Clerk, WorkOS AuthKit, Auth0, and custom JWT providers.
Explains how to configure Convex auth files and client provider wrappers.
Shows how to protect Convex queries and mutations with server-side identity checks.
Covers optional user records, token identity mapping, roles, and ownership checks.
Includes validation steps for local and production authentication setup.
limitations: Only applies to applications that use Convex as a backend.
Provider dashboards, tenant creation, and login prompts may require human action.
Official provider documentation remains the source of truth for changing setup flows.
Production validation requires access to real deployment, redirect, and credential settings.

use_cases.title: Launch authentication in a new Convex app
use_cases.description: Select a provider, initialize required files, wire the client provider, and verify a first login flow.

use_cases.title: Connect an existing identity provider
use_cases.description: Integrate Clerk, Auth0, or WorkOS AuthKit with Convex while preserving existing provider configuration.

use_cases.title: Harden backend access control
use_cases.description: Replace client-supplied user identifiers with server-side identity checks and role or ownership rules.

prompt_templates.title: Add basic Convex Auth
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Convex application.
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up authentication for this Convex app using Convex Auth. Ask me which sign-in methods I want, then implement the smallest working local setup.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate Clerk with Convex
prompt_templates.scenario: Existing frontend app that should use Clerk sessions with Convex.
prompt_templates.prompt: Add Clerk authentication to this Convex app. Check the framework, wire the providers, configure Convex token validation, and give me the validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare production auth settings
prompt_templates.scenario: Production-focused setup that needs provider and deployment checks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Convex authentication setup for production readiness. Check environment variables, redirect URLs, provider tenant settings, and backend auth configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Audit authorization boundaries
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of data access after authentication is configured.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my Convex queries and mutations for authorization gaps. Replace client-trusted user identifiers with identity-based checks and recommend role or ownership rules.

output_examples.input: Set up login for my new Convex app.
output: Asks which provider and sign-in methods the app should use.
Initializes the chosen provider setup and identifies generated files.
Lists follow-up validation steps for sign in, sign out, and protected data access.

output_examples.input: My app already uses Clerk. Make Convex require login.
output: Finds existing Clerk wiring and preserves current application structure.
Configures Convex token validation and auth-aware provider wrapping.
Adds backend identity checks before protected queries and mutations return data.

output_examples.input: Check whether my Convex auth setup is production ready.
output: Reviews provider-specific production settings and redirect URLs.
Checks whether backend auth configuration has been pushed to the target deployment.
Reports any remaining human steps for dashboard or credential configuration.

best_practices: Choose the auth provider before editing code or installing packages.
Use server-side Convex identity checks instead of trusting client-provided user IDs.
Validate both local and production provider settings when production readiness is requested.
anti_patterns: Do not invent a shared users table flow for every provider without checking the app need.
Do not treat a successful frontend login as proof that Convex can validate the session.
Do not reuse development provider credentials or redirect URLs in production without verification.

faq.question: Which auth providers does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Convex Auth, Clerk, WorkOS AuthKit, Auth0, and custom JWT provider setup for Convex applications.

faq.question: Does it create user records automatically?
faq.answer: It recommends user records only when the app needs first-class user data inside Convex.

faq.question: Can it configure production authentication?
faq.answer: Yes, when deployment details, provider settings, redirect URLs, and credentials are available.

faq.question: Will it need access to secrets?
faq.answer: Some provider setup requires environment variables or dashboard values. Secrets should remain in approved local or deployment configuration.

faq.question: Can it verify the login flow?
faq.answer: It can guide or run validation when browser automation and provider access are available.

faq.question: Is it useful for non-Convex backends?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is specific to Convex authentication and authorization patterns.
